slides.title: Taking out the trash 🗑️
slides.content:
# Erasing History

Sometimes, data needs to be permanently removed. If a user deletes their account, we shouldn't keep their personal data in our `users` table.

To physically wipe a row from existence, we use the `DELETE FROM` statement.

slides.title: The syntax ⚔️
slides.content:
## Striking it down

Deleting is very straightforward.
`DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 10;`

That row is instantly evaporated. There is no undo button in standard SQL.

quizData.question: Which properly formatted statement deletes user #10?
quizData.explanation: DELETE inherently requires the word FROM to designate which targeted table the deletion originates in.

slides.title: Deleting vs Dropping 🧱
slides.content:
## Rows vs Tables

`DELETE` ONLY ever targets individual internal rows of data inside an existing table.

It does not delete the physical structural Table itself. The 'box' remains securely in the database, you've just emptied its contents.

quizData.question: If you successfully execute a massive DELETE statement on your 'users' table, does the 'users' table itself get destroyed?

options.text: Yes, if it empties, the table auto-deletes
No, DELETE exclusively targets internal rows (data), never the structural table schema itself.

quizData.explanation: DELETE handles the 'contents' of the box, not the box itself.
